This is incorrect, the `uri` we're returning is derived from the rope resource (line above), however this version number is derived from the document within which the rename was triggered.

If you check the LSP, version is not a required field: https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/specification#versionedtextdocumentidentifier

However, we can do better than just never setting it. If you use `workspace.get_document(doc_uri)` and then use that document's version then it will return None (correctly) if the server has never received a didOpen message, or the correct version number if it has. 

My concern with doing this is that rope doesn't operate on the in-memory view of the documents. We should probably fix that before making too heavy usage of rope. In particular, the edits returned by rope could be inconsistent with the version of the document.

It's both a future proof thing and an easy way to define which signers we support in Botocore. If we find one of the preferred signers in the list of signature versions, then we know that we can send requests to a service.

Checking in a predefined order is a future proof thing. The reason to check for v4 and prefer it over v2 is to correctly handle the case of one of the few remaining v2 services adding support for v4. At that point, it is unlikely the service would stop supporting v2, so we'd need to account for the case of both v2 and v4 as available signature versions.

> I just meant I wanted to use ANY as the sentinel value, and not mix in {} and ANY as a sentinel value.

I think it's better to have an API that doesn't even need a sentinel value, period. I'm not saying that this should be the case at all costs. But if Python code does have a sentinel value, it's almost always None, and if it's not, it's not something that needs to be passed in explicitly by the user (i.e. it may be a default argument to a function that the user can ignore). So here's my kind of hierarchy of desirability with respect to function parameters:

1) no sentinel values needed
2) implicit sentinel value of None
3) explicit sentinel value of None
4) implicit non-None sentinel value
5) explicit non-None sentinel value

I think the API will be easy to understand if described in terms of what checks are done. Right now, the SequenceStringStub docstring doesn't mention `ANY` as a valid value (and if we continue doing this `ANY` identity checks, it definitely should). It also doesn't mention how the tuple elements are interpreted. Let me propose a change.

>    :ivar list sequence: A sequence of tuples of (method, url, params, headers_subset, data), where all elements is checked for equality with the actual request parameters, except for `headers_subset`, which is checked to be a subset of the actual headers. The method should be lowercase.

By reading this docstring, it should be pretty apparent that you can't use ANY for `headers_subset` since it doesn't say it's checked for equality. Even if it's not, and you try to use `ANY`, the test will fail immediately on comparing the headers so it should be pretty easy to realize what the problem is.

Right now the docstring doesn't mention that `ANY` is explicitly checked, so it's already confusing. If I know ANY is an object equal to all values, then I would not expect it to work when passed as `headers_subset`, since I know that headers_subset is *not* checked for equality.

Lastly: if I'm using `mock.ANY`, I would expect to be able to pass it in the same situations as this `ANY` object. So if we do stick with an explicit sentinel, I think it'd be better to call it something else, like MATCH_ALL or something. 

> As for why we are round tripping the expected URL, I was hoping to throw away extra things like query args, a double slash, etc. which URLPath normalizes, but maybe it's better to not do that so its more plain that the user provided the wrong expected value.

Yeah, I agree. If it's test code, it should be pretty precise.

> I think that while we should accept {} for headers though, it would also make sense to still accept ANY? Otherwise it would be the one item in the tuple that ANY can't be used for.

I think it's fine that `ANY` can't be used for headers as long as you stop thinking of `ANY` as an explicit "sentinel value" and more as a "thing that is equal to all things", which is what it's currently documented as.

As a final point, I'm also not totally convinced that we should really be using HasHeaders here. Of course, it would simplify the code to an equality check, which makes this API design simpler, but it also means that users would be able to perform negative tests on their code. If I have an application that *sometimes* requests things with the `X-CreditCardNumber`, then I can very easily see myself writing tests that try to ensure that `X-CreditCardNumber` is *not* passed unless it's strictly needed. glyph says that treq reserves the right to start passing whatever headers it wants, but if it starts passing `X-CreditCardNumber` then I'd also very much like my tests to start failing :)

So if we get rid of the required use of `HasHeaders`, then you get the ability to pass `ANY` as the expected headers field, *and* you get the ability to pass in any other equality-overriding predicate you want, or just a simple, complete value of expected headers.

If you really want to guarantee backwards compatibility, retain the same ordering of all the arguments that existed previously and add your new one at the end:

```
def download_dxfile(dxid, filename, chunksize=dxfile.DEFAULT_BUFFER_SIZE, append=False, show_progress=False,
                    project=None, dxfile=None, **kwargs):
``

However, both this and the proposed API above are kind of annoying to use. `dxid` and `dxfile` are mutually exclusive, but if you supply `dxfile` as a keyword argument you then still have to override `dxid=None`. One option is to make all the arguments keyword:

```
def download_dxfile(dxid=None, filename=None, ..., dxfile=None, **kwargs):
```

But I find this kind of unsatisfying too since you lose the statically available information that at least two arguments are needed. Frankly I'd be fine with restoring the previous signature exactly and just documenting the fact that you can supply a DXFile or an ID in the `dxid` position. I wonder if that may be overstating things a bit. As I understand it, this simply drops top-level `__annotations__` completely from the class at runtime. It allows Attribute values to thus be annotated in the source, but that's about it. For something that evaluates the AST (mypy?) that's probably OK, but for something that uses that data at runtime, like the stdlib `typing` module, that's not exactly "supported". (Indeed, `tpying.get_type_hints` raises an exception when passed an interface: `TypeError: <InterfaceClass __main__.IFoo> is not a module, class, method, or function.`)

In addition, methods also still completely lose their annotations (because they're represented by a custom type). E.g., this works with the released versions of zope.interface today:
```python
class IFoo(Interface):
   def thing(s:str) -> str:
       pass
```

But `IFoo['thing']` has no `__annotations__`.

It's okay that it's racy. The resolution is to just keep the generator open. The idea is that there are three states:

1. Generator created, rpc not set.
2. gRPC started a thread to consume the generator, rpc is still not set.
3. rpc is set, now start monitoring it to see when it stops.

So in all of those cases the right thing to do is to just keep the generator open.

The point of this is to give conda 4.4 the behavior conda 4.3 and earlier had.  If you had a directory `C:\Users\build er\Miniconda3`, and were to do something like

    %windir%\system32\cmd.exe "/K" "C:\Users\build er\Miniconda3\Scripts\activate.bat" "C:\Users\build er\Miniconda3"

and also put `echo %*` at the top of `activate.bat`, you get 

    "C:\Users\build

as output.  Which is total crap.  But conda 4.3 and earlier just swallowed that, and activated the root environment.  That's all we're trying to accomplish here.

I ended up doing it a bit differently:

```
class dicttransform(
        namedtuple('dicttransform', ['old_key', 'new_key', 'transform'])):
    '''Helper data structure used to simplify the transformations on data when
    parsing the object from JSON. Used in conjunction with `transform_dict`
    :param str old_key: Source key
    :param str new_key: Destination key. If not specified same as `old_key``
    :param function transform: Function used to transform the data, if not
                               specified uses identity function
    Example usage:
    dicttransform('old_key', 'new_key') - renames keys
    dicttransform('old_key', transform=some_function)
     - uses some_function to transform data at `old_key`
    dicttransform('old_key', 'new_key', some_function)
     - use some_function to transform the data and rename the key from
       `old_key` to `new_key`
    '''

    def __new__(cls, old_key, new_key=None, transform=lambda x: x):
        if new_key is None:
            new_key = old_key

        return super(dicttransform, cls).__new__(cls, old_key, new_key,
                                                 transform)


def transform_dict(data, *key_transformations):
    '''
    :param dict data: Dictionary to be transformed
    :param list(dicttransform): Transformation to perform
    '''
    new_data = dict(data)
    for old_key, new_key, transform in key_transformations:
        if old_key in new_data:
            old_value = new_data.pop(old_key)
            new_data[new_key] = transform(old_value)
    return new_data
```

As there is a case for:
1. Renaming the key and keeping the value as it was
2. Processing the value (keeping the key name)
3. Both 1 and 2, process the value and rename the key

```
old_func_symbol = self.loader.find_symbol(symbol_name)

2- as soon as we read an address of a resolved function we can implement an hack in order to find the base address of the library by reading backward at multiple of pages until we find the ELF/PE header. This PR is part of performance and battery consumption optimization for DA15000. Correct me if I am wrong, but most of other examples does not fully cover that topic. I have seen examples providing support by HW crypto functions, which will be also introduce in our next upcoming PR, but this particular PR is rather connected to battery consumption topic. You might see this topic as increasing CPU clock for DTLS operations, but our original idea was to safe battery by decreasing CPU clock frequency everywhere it is possible. From that perspective we do not treat it as Commissioner and Joiner issue. Those are cases were high performance is mandatory and due to that cannot or we just do not see a point to take care about battery devices. 

Commissioner with battery is rather not good idea, so that why we do not care so much about it and we do not need to slow down CPU for that case. In case of SED device which will need to act for a short time as JOINER it is much important to limit CPU usage as much as possible.

I would agree that CPU_HIGH_POWER might be not the best names, but  names OT_CHANGED_COMMISSIONER_STATE or OT_CHANGED JOINER_STATE will hide the overall idea of this PR. It would be great if other platform could utilize this approach and optimize battery consumption by that. In the worst scenario this change on OpenThread code would be done just for DA15000 purpose.

What do you think about HIGH_PERFORMANCE_NEEDED and BATTERY_SAVING_POSSIBLE states? Someone could complain that, those might not representing real states, but it will shows the idea of this PR.

I would feel differently if pre and post hooks before this PR already ran in a last-in, first-out order. That's not what we do though. When `certbot renew` is run, pre-hooks for each lineage are run in the order they are encountered and the same is done for post-hooks. Each post-hook is saved and they are run before Certbot exits in the order they were found.

A similar argument could (and maybe should) have been made before we shipped the existing hooks system. Pre and post hooks from different configs could also interact with each other in strange ways.

We have the system we currently have though and while I know you're not proposing changing this part, I think it changing the order like this adds some (minor) additional complexity and inconsistency to our hooks. It's another detail that has to be explained and understood. It's an exception to the simple explanation "directory hooks run before config hooks".

I don't have a super strong opinion here, but I do have a preference for what I have in this PR.

@ohemorange, do you have an opinion here? If I'm in the minority here, I'll happily make this change.
